# PPTX Skill

## Quick Reference

| Task | Guide |
|------|-------|
| Read/analyze content | `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` |
| Edit or create from template | Read [editing.md](editing.md) |
| Create from scratch | Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) |

---

## Reading Content

```bash
# Text extraction
python -m markitdown presentation.pptx

# Visual overview
python scripts/thumbnail.py presentation.pptx

# Raw XML
python scripts/office/unpack.py presentation.pptx unpacked/
```

---

## Editing Workflow

**Read [editing.md](editing.md) for full details.**

1. Analyze template with `thumbnail.py`
2. Unpack → manipulate slides → edit content → clean → pack

---

## Creating from Scratch

**Read [pptxgenjs.md](pptxgenjs.md) for full details.**

Use when no template or reference presentation is available.

---

## Design Ideas

**Don't create boring slides.** Plain bullets on a white background won't impress anyone. Consider ideas from this list for each slide.

### Before Starting

- **Pick a bold, content-informed color palette**: The palette should feel designed for THIS topic. If swapping your colors into a completely different presentation would still "work," you haven't made specific enough choices.
- **Dominance over equality**: One color should dominate (60-70% visual weight), with 1-2 supporting tones and one sharp accent. Never give all colors equal weight.
- **Dark/light contrast**: Dark backgrounds for title + conclusion slides, light for content ("sandwich" structure). Or commit to dark throughout for a premium feel.
- **Commit to a visual motif**: Pick ONE distinctive element and repeat it — rounded image frames, icons in colored circles, thick single-side borders. Carry it across every slide.

### Color Palettes

Choose colors that match your topic — don't default to generic blue. Use these palettes as inspiration:

| Theme | Primary | Secondary | Accent |
|-------|---------|-----------|--------|
| **Midnight Executive** | `1E2761` (navy) | `CADCFC` (ice blue) | `FFFFFF` (white) |
| **Forest & Moss** | `2C5F2D` (forest) | `97BC62` (moss) | `F5F5F5` (cream) |
| **Coral Energy** | `F96167` (coral) | `F9E795` (gold) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |
| **Warm Terracotta** | `B85042` (terracotta) | `E7E8D1` (sand) | `A7BEAE` (sage) |
| **Ocean Gradient** | `065A82` (deep blue) | `1C7293` (teal) | `21295C` (midnight) |
| **Charcoal Minimal** | `36454F` (charcoal) | `F2F2F2` (off-white) | `212121` (black) |
| **Teal Trust** | `028090` (teal) | `00A896` (seafoam) | `02C39A` (mint) |
| **Berry & Cream** | `6D2E46` (berry) | `A26769` (dusty rose) | `ECE2D0` (cream) |
| **Sage Calm** | `84B59F` (sage) | `69A297` (eucalyptus) | `50808E` (slate) |
| **Cherry Bold** | `990011` (cherry) | `FCF6F5` (off-white) | `2F3C7E` (navy) |

### For Each Slide

**Every slide needs a visual element** — image, chart, icon, or shape. Text-only slides are forgettable.

**Layout options:**
- Two-column (text left, illustration on right)
- Icon + text rows (icon in colored circle, bold header, description below)
- 2x2 or 2x3 grid (image on one side, grid of content blocks on other)
- Half-bleed image (full left or right side) with content overlay

**Data display:**
- Large stat callouts (big numbers 60-72pt with small labels below)
- Comparison columns (before/after, pros/cons, side-by-side options)
- Timeline or process flow (numbered steps, arrows)

**Visual polish:**
- Icons in small colored circles next to section headers
- Italic accent text for key stats or taglines

### Typography

**Choose an interesting font pairing** — don't default to Arial. Pick a header font with personality and pair it with a clean body font.

| Header Font | Body Font |
|-------------|-----------|
| Georgia | Calibri |
| Arial Black | Arial |
| Calibri | Calibri Light |
| Cambria | Calibri |
| Trebuchet MS | Calibri |
| Impact | Arial |
| Palatino | Garamond |
| Consolas | Calibri |

The project is about rebuilding ByteDance's Identity Graph from Connected Components to a probabilistic system with message passing. Key facts:
- 25B edges, 22B serving rows, <12h SLA
- Two-phase algorithm: edge probability precomputation (logistic function) + message passing (top-K propagation)
- Cross-references 12+ data sources (1P: NPS, contact sync, device, IPv6, TTS; 2P: click-ID, cookie, advertiser agreement; 3P: Adstra, Experian, TransUnion)
- Weight learning: started with manual intuition, moved to logistic regression with click-ID ground truth
- Score threshold 0.6, email accuracy >74%, phone >71%
- GraphX Pregel OOMed, pivoted to SQL-based message passing (2-week setback)
- v2 optimization: frontier nodes only, bucketed intermediary tables
- 6+ versions iterated (v0-v5)
- Online serving: Abase key-value, 22B rows at 600k QPS
- Known limitation: single-path probability (max not union), evidence independence assumption
- Downstream consumers: DM, PM, Lift, Custom Audiences, xDM/UM, Global Identity

Structure the file with these sections:
1. "Why This Design?" questions (algorithm choice, logistic vs Bayesian, message passing vs alternatives)
2. "What Were the Main Tradeoffs?" questions (single-path, independence, Person ID, coverage vs accuracy)
3. "What Went Wrong in Production?" questions (GraphX OOM, scaling issues, data quality, compliance)
4. "How Did You Measure Success?" questions (ground truth choice, metrics, evaluation methodology)
5. Technical Deep-Dive questions (supernodes, hop count, numerical stability, weight learning)
6. System Design questions (online serving, cross-DC sync, disaster recovery, data pipeline)
7. Leadership & Staff-Level questions (cross-team coordination, stakeholder management, scoping)

For each question, provide:
- The question itself
- Key talking points (2-4 bullets)
- A "watch out for" note about common interviewer follow-ups

Write it as a practical interview prep document. Use clear markdown formatting. This is for a Staff Engineer level interview at a top AI company.

### 2. Add a NEW slide after current slide 13 (Iterative Evaluation) — "Optimization Deep Dive"
This slide should explain the v1→v2 optimization journey in more detail:

Title: "Optimization Deep Dive: v1 → v2"
Layout: Dark background (C.navy), two-column layout

Left column "v1: Baseline Implementation":
- Generate unique bigint IDs for every vertex: 1h
- Generate bi-directional edges: 10m  
- Generate vertex table: 14m
- Each hop: ~1h → Total: ~5.3h for 5 hops
- Resources: 500 cores, 15 TB memory
- Problem: Limited by memory, full graph reprocessed each hop

Right column "v2: Frontier Optimization":
- Key insight: only join on "frontier" nodes (vertices whose TopK changed)
- Save intermediary hop results in bucketed tables
- Bucketing on identifier reduces Spark shuffle dramatically
- Removes need for bigint ID assignment entirely
- Each hop uses predictable, bounded time and resources
- Next-step optimizations considered: index, skip unchanged vertices, intermediary tables

Bottom callout: "Numerical Stability" box explaining log-probs: log(Π pᵢ) = −Σ log(1+e^(−zᵢ)), logsumexp trick for future negative weights

### 3. Add presenter notes (s.addNotes("...")) to EVERY slide

The scale is significant: 25 billion edges, 22 billion rows in the online serving table, running under a 12-hour SLA."

**Slide 2 (Section 1 divider)**: "Let me start with the problem context."

**Slide 3 (Problem Context)**: "When an advertiser runs a campaign on TikTok, they send conversion events — purchases, sign-ups — with user identifiers like emails, phone numbers, or device IDs. Our job is to match those identifiers to TikTok user accounts so we can attribute the conversion to the right ad. The identity graph does that mapping. Your interview playbook is ready. Here's the quick strategy to pass this:

**The 60-minute game plan:**
- **First 10 minutes (intro + high-level):** You set the frame. If you nail the intro, interviewers relax and let you drive. Memorize the opening script verbatim — it's the only part you should rehearse word-for-word.
- **Next 15 minutes (deep dive):** Budget it tightly — 3 min architecture, 3 min scoring, 3 min weights, 3 min scaling, 3 min eval. If they interrupt early, abandon your plan and follow their curiosity. That's a good sign.
- **Last 35 minutes (Q&A):** This is where staff-level is decided. Expect 7–12 questions. Use **STAR+L** (Situation, Task, Action, Result, **Learning**) — the "what I'd do differently" line is what separates senior from staff.

**Top 3 things to internalize before game day:**
1. Say "**I**" for decisions, "**we**" for execution. Staff-level interviewers are listening for ownership.
2. When asked "why not X?" — **steelman their idea first**, then defend yours with a cost/benefit frame tied to the state of the team at that time.
3. Every answer should end in a way that makes them want to **hire you for judgment**, not just expertise.

Now you're onto something. "Users have goals but exploring plans hurts" — that's a genuine pain, and AI is genuinely the right lever for it. Let me push on it before we converge.

## What's strong (don't second-guess)

- **"Start small, grow on success"** is the single best psychological mechanic you could pick. Fogg's Tiny Habits, Atomic Habits, progressive overload all say the same thing. Most goal apps fail by being too ambitious on day 1.
- **Failure → ask why → adjust plan** is the loop that separates "coach" from "tracker." 99% of habit apps don't do this — they just break your streak and shame you. If you nail this, you have a real moat.
- **Solo-first** is the right call. Family-shared AI plans get socially weird fast. Nail the single-user loop, let family come back in V3 as "share a plan I'm running."

## Where the WOW actually lives

It's not "AI-native" in the abstract. The specific wow moment is:

> **"I type one sentence. 90 seconds later, I have tomorrow's single first action that feels like a smart friend planned it."**

Everything else — the plan doc, the recurrence, the adjustments — is scaffolding. If this first session doesn't land, nothing else matters.

Corollary: your first-session onboarding is the entire product. Not the daily check-in. Not the dashboard. The 90 seconds after the user types their goal. Design that first, everything else second.

## Five hard questions you haven't answered yet

**1. What exactly does the AI ask?** You said "LLM ask user more questions to make good plans." For "I want to keep fit in 3 months," write down the *actual* 3-5 questions you'd ask. Right now. In Chinese.

If you can't, the UX isn't designed. "AI asks smart questions" is vaporware until you've written the questions. My hunch: for fit, you need roughly — current activity baseline, time per day, equipment access, injury/constraints, primary motivation (lose weight vs feel stronger vs look different). For Japanese: current level, minutes per day, reading vs speaking priority, deadline flexibility. Three goals = three interview scripts. Hand-write them before you code.

**2. Why a "doc"?** Your step 4 says "create the entire plan in doc but only surface small calendar todo." Two possibilities:
- **Doc = visible plan** user can open/read/edit → another surface, UX complexity
- **Doc = AI's internal scratchpad** → shouldn't be called a doc; it's a data structure

Pick one. If it's option 1, the doc IS a feature and deserves design. If it's 2, delete the word "doc" from your spec.

My opinion: the user SHOULD be able to see the full plan at will ("what's this whole thing leading to?"), but not forced to read it. Optional transparency. Hide by default, reveal on tap.

**3. What is "perfect execution" and how is failure handled?** "If user executes perfectly, create more" — is that 100% of today's items? Or 80%? Do you measure on a rolling window? And when they fail — what's the tone?

The failure conversation is the single UX moment that will make or break this product. Two wrong tones I'd avoid:
- **Concerned-nurse**: "Is everything OK? You missed yesterday." — feels like being monitored
- **Drill-sergeant**: "You missed 2/3. Let's get back on track." — feels like being judged

Right tone, guessing: **curious-friend** — "Yesterday slipped. Want to adjust, or just skip it?" No guilt, gives user agency, treats the plan as a draft not a contract.

**4. How many active plans?** One at a time (forced focus), or many (dashboard)?

My opinion: **one primary plan, one optional background habit**. Primary = "the thing I'm working on this quarter" (fit, language, etc). Background = "drink 2L water" ambient habit. Any more and the daily list becomes overwhelming, and you're back to Todoist.

**5. What happens to the 8 fixed health habits you already built?**

Three options:
- Delete them; everyone starts from goal-entry screen
- Keep them as a pre-seeded "wellness cadence" plan any user can adopt
- Make them specifically YOUR plan

## The daily loop — completion logic

```
今日完成率:
  ≥ 80%  → 🎯 "节奏很稳，明天继续"  + 解锁下一 batch (AI 生成未来 3-7 天)
  60-80% → ✓  "今天做得不错"         + 继续当前 batch
  < 60%  → 🫂 "今天有点满？告诉我什么挡住你了：
              [太累] [时间不够] [没兴趣做这个] [别的事]
              → AI 消化理由，下一 batch 调整(降强度/换项目/间隔延长)
```

Curious-friend tone throughout. No streak-breaking shame. If user skips 3 days, plan pauses itself and asks if they want to reduce scope.

**"I don't need to select if I already on it"** — the onboarding skips questions we already have data for. User has been checking 💧 water + 😴 sleep for a week → form doesn't ask "want water/sleep as background?" It auto-includes them with an inline "保留" toggle.

- Floating button (bottom-right on /today, or in header). Tap → drawer slides up.
- Chat with plan in context (prompt-cached so it's fast + cheap).
- Coach can propose plan edits. When it does, a **diff card** appears in-chat:
  ```
  建议改动:
    · 把周三的 HIIT 换成瑜伽(你说过膝盖疼)
    · 第3周加一次力量训练
    [应用] [再想想]
  ```
- User taps apply → plan doc + future `plan_items` regenerate. Past completions stay intact.

This is the crucial integration — the coach isn't passive. It WRITES to your plan.

### The headline findings from the competitive brief

- **Empty quadrant**: low-friction × high-plan-intelligence is genuinely unoccupied. Every AI coach is chat-first (Rocky, Ash, ChatGPT — high friction). Every low-friction habit app has zero AI (Streaks, Finch, Stoic). Fabulous is the nearest neighbor but uses curated routines, not LLM generation. **This is Cadence's actual wedge.**
- **Biggest threat: Fabulous adds LLM plan generation.** 2-month move for them. They'd own your onboarding pitch with more brand and retention behind them.
- **Second threat: ChatGPT Projects/Memory** — for users already in ChatGPT, your value prop is replicable with zero install. Don't try to win them; focus on the 95% who don't live in a chat window.
- **CN wedge is real and underrated.** Every US AI coach is English-only. Keep/薄荷 do plans vertically. Nobody does horizontal goal coaching in ZH. Make CN the distribution story.
- **"Curious friend" tone is the most commoditized-sounding axis and the hardest to actually ship** — every competitor says they're warm and encouraging, and almost none are. Ship tone regression tests before you ship the coach.

1. **Obsess over first-90s plan quality** — hand-write 20 canonical first plans before writing generation prompts; use as eval suite; keep 3-variant sampling even though it doubles cost. If the first plan feels generic, nothing else matters.
2. **Ship CN-first explicitly** — default language stays `zh`, archetypes include 考研/减脂/考公/雅思/学日语, tone tuned for CN gen-z / millennial warmth. This is your distribution wedge.

### Latest GLM models (April 2026, confirmed from Zhipu/Z.ai pricing pages)

| Model | Cost (per MTok) | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **GLM-5** / GLM-5.1 | $1.00 / $1.40 input | Frontier; rivals Opus on SWE-Bench Pro |
| **GLM-4.7** | $0.60 / $2.20 | Mid-tier paid |

| **GLM-4.5** | $0.60 / $2.20 | Cheap structured output |

### What I wired

- **`CADENCE_LLM_MODE=testing`** (default) routes plan-gen + selector through **GLM-5**, everything else through **GLM-4.5-Flash** (free). Set `CADENCE_LLM_MODE=production` to flip plan-gen back to Claude Opus.
- **Auto-JWT wrapping** — Zhipu's `{id}.{secret}` key format requires a custom HS256 JWT (ms-based `exp`, `sign_type: SIGN` header). `getGlmClient()` signs it automatically, refreshes every 55 min. Z.ai-issued raw tokens pass through unchanged.
- Schema + prompts shared across both providers — same `save_plan` / `pick_winner` tool-call contract.
